Facts.text: St. Caius begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Ostrogoths of King Totila conquer Rome by bribing the Byzantine garrison during the Gothic War
Romanos I is crowned as co-emperor of the underage Emperor Constantine VII
Assassination of William I Longsword, 2nd Duke of Normandy.
Crusader Wallon de Sarton presents Bishop of Amiens Richard de Gerberoy with purported skull of John the Baptist, stolen from Constantinople (Amiens cathedral later built to house it)
Ferdinand of Austria chosen as King of Bohemia
Spanish army starts fires in Haarlem, Netherlands
Go-Yōzei becomes 107th Emperor of Japan
The Earl of Leicester's army leaves Netherlands
Shimabara Rebellion: Japanese peasants led by Amakusa Shiro rise against daimyo Matsukura Shigeharu
French and Swedish troops occupy Breisach on the Rhine
France, Britain and Austria declare war on Spain (War of the Quadruple Alliance 1718–1720)
Congregation Shearith Israel of New York purchases a lot on Mill Street in Lower Manhattan to build the city's first synagogue
France recognizes independence of British colonies in America
George Washington's army returns to Valley Forge, Pennsylvania
Russian army led by Grigory Potemkin assaults and captures the fortress of Ochakov in modern-day Ukraine after a five-month-long siege in one of the major events of the Russo-Turkish War
Aztec calendar stone discovered in Mexico City
NYC traffic regulation creates 1st 1-way street
Opening of 1st legislative assembly of Lower Canada in Quebec city
1st impeachment trial against a US senator (William Blount, Tennessee) begins
France issues the Milan Decree, which confirms the Continental System.
Congress of Angostura establishes Colombia's independence from Spain
Kentucky abolishes debtors' prisons

Real Estate developer Samuel Ruggles deeds Gramercy Park to board of trustees to hold as parkland, he is granted tax exempt status by New York City for the park, and develops the surrounding lots ensuring private access only to those properties
Colonel William Light chooses the site for the settlement of Adelaide in South Australia
Henry Cole, founder of London's V&A Museum, commissions printing of the 1st Christmas card
1st Hawaiian cavalry organized
Anaheim Township created in Los Angeles County
Violent bread riots in Montreal
France declares Madagascar a protectorate
Georges Feydeau's "Tailleur Pour Dames" premieres in Paris
Tchaikovski's ballet "Casse-noisette" premieres in St Petersburg
Russia ratifies Double Alliance with France
Day 3 of first cricket test between Australia and England, England 325 all out, 261 behind on 1st innings
Anti-Saloon League of America formed, Washington, D.C.
George Brownell patents a machine to make paper twine (Mass)
First prize of 100,000 francs is offered for communication with extraterrestrials, with Martians excluded as they are considered too easy
New Ellis Island Immigration station completed costing $1.5 million
Ugyen Wangchuck became 1st hereditary king of Bhutan
Albania's de facto independence from the Ottoman Empire is recognized at the London Conference of Ambassadors
Austrian troops beat Russians in Limanova Poland
Great Britain declares Egypt a protectorate
Jews are expelled from Tel Aviv by Turkish authorities
A conference of workers' and soldiers' councils take control of German government in Berlin, until elections in January selects new national assembly
Austrian parliament approves 8-hour working day
AL votes to let spitball pitchers to continue using it
British Empire receives League of Nations mandate over Nauru
Japan receives League of Nations mandate over Pacific islands
South Africa receives League of Nations mandate over SW Africa
Last British troops leave Irish Free State
1st US diesel electric locomotive enters service, Bronx, NY
The Hamilton Tigers and Ottawa Senators battle out the first scoreless game in NHL history; Jake Forbes (Hamilton) and Alex Connell (Ottawa) each record shutouts
Colonel William "Billy" Mitchell court-martialed for insubordination
Soviet Union & Turkey sign non-aggresion pact
Antanas Smetona assumes power in Lithuania as the ‎1926 coup d'état‎ is successful
German Marx government falls due to cooperation with red army
KYA-AM in San Francisco CA begins radio transmissions
Lithuanian military state under gen Augustine Woldemaras
US submarine S-4 sinks after collision killing all 34 aboard
John McGraw backs NL President John Heydler's designated hitter idea
Bryan Valentine (136) and Lala Amarnath (102no) score centuries on their Test Cricket debut on Day 3 of 1st Test between England and India at Bombay
Spain's 2nd government of Lerroux forms
Ventriloquist Edgar Bergen and dummy Charlie McCarthy appear on TV
Discovery of nuclear fission using uranium by Otto Hahn and his assistant Fritz Strassmann
Utrecht Central Station destroyed by fire
German pocket battleship Graf Spee scuttled by its crew off Uruguay
British troops occupy Sollum, Egypt
Dutch and Australian troops land on Portuguese Timor
German submarine U-31 sunk
World War II: Beginning of the Siege of Sebastopol.
Allies in London sentence German war criminals
Transport 63 departs with French Jews to nazi-Germany
San Francisco M-Ocean View streetcar resumes service & is extended to Market St
US Army announces end of excluding Japanese-Americans from West Coast, detainees released
US destroyers sink in storm off Philippines, 790 killed
Bradman& Barnes complete record 405 run 5th wicket stand, score 234 each in 2nd Cricket Test Australia vs. England in Sydney
US V-2 rocket reaches 183 km, White Sands Proving Grounds, New Mexico
NY struck by a blizzard, resulting with 27" of snow
WEWS TV channel 5 in Cleveland, Ohio (ABC) begins broadcasting
Burma recognizes People's Republic of China
Dutch Communist Party members forbidden to be civil servants
US Federal Communications Commission approves RCA's black & white-compatible color TV specifications
1st fully automated railroad freight yard (Gary, Indiana)
WEAU TV channel 13 in Eau Claire, WI (NBC) begins broadcasting

The largest trade in NHL history at the time in terms of quantity; Chicago Black Hawks move Nick Mickoski, Jack McIntyre, Bob Bailey & Hec Lalonde to Detroit Red Wings for Earl Reibel, Billy Dea, Bill Dineen & Lorne Ferguson
US successfully test-fires Atlas intercontinental ballistic missile
"On The Beach" is 1st film to premiere on both sides of Iron Curtain
Pablo Casals' oratorio "El Pesebrio" premieres in Acapulco, Mexico
India seizes Goa & 2 other Portuguese colonies
Niteroi Circus in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil catches fire; 323 die after disgruntled employee sets fire to the tent
Beatles 1st British TV appearance on "People & Places"
Current constitution of Monaco promulgated

Ballon d'Or: Dynamo Moscow goalkeeper Lev Yashin wins award for best European football player; beats Milan midfielder Gianni Rivera and Tottenham striker Jimmy Greaves; first Russian national to win trophy
Tsjoi Doo Sun forms government in South Korea
West & East Berlin sign accord about travel rules
British government proclaims ends oil-embargo against Rhodesia
David Levy begins his search for comets
Dutch government shuts Limburgs coal mine
Largest newspaper-Sunday NY Times at 946 pages (50 cents)
Harold Holt, Prime Minister of Australia, vanishes in mysterious circumstances while swimming near Melbourne
WEDW TV channel 49 in Bridgeport, Connecticut (PBS) begins broadcasting
USAF closes Project Blue Book, concluding no evidence of extraterrestrial spaceships behind thousands of UFO sightings
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Workers returning to shipyard in Gdynia, Poland are met by soldiers who shoot at the crowd, killing at least 18, wounding hundreds
"Diamonds are Forever", 7th James Bond film, last starring Sean Conner, also starring Jill St. John is 1st released in West Germany
Cease fire between India & Pakistan in Kashmir
Radio Bangladesh begins transmitting
New line of control agreed to in Kashmir between India & Pakistan
WGVC (now WUCX) TV channel 35 in Grand Rapids, MI (PBS) 1st broadcast
Arabs terrorists shoot passengers on Boeing 737 to Kuwait
John Paul Stevens appointed to the US Supreme Court
Lynette Fromme sentenced to life for attempt on US President Ford's life
NHL NY Islanders shut-out NY Rangers, for 1st time in 3-0 win with Billy Smith in goal
NYC's Grand Central Terminal train station is added to the National Register of Historic Places
Superstation WTBS in Atlanta went national
Bobby Simpson scores 176 Australia v India at the WACA, aged 41
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll

Rookie defenseman Barry Beck scores first career hat trick and adds an assist in Colorado Rockies' 5-1 win v Minnesota North Stars; sets NHL record for rookie defensemen with a goal in his 6th straight game
OPEC raises oil prices by 18% and agrees on a 14.5% petroleum price increase for 1979, to be implemented quarterly
Referendum approves new constitution of Rwanda
The Workers' Party of Jamaica is founded by Trevor Munroe
Budweiser rocket car reaches 1190 kph (record for wheeled vehicle, disputed due to measurement methodology used)
Great Britain performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Mauritania provisional constitution published
Members of Red Brigades kidnap Brigadier General James L Dozier
Disco in Madrid catches fire; 83 die
The Provisional IRA bombs Harrods department store in London, killing six people and injuring 90
Davina Thompson makes medical history by having the first heart, lung, and liver transplant at Papworth Hospital in Cambridge, England

Edmonton'sWayne Gretzkyscores 4 goals, including a natural hat trick in the 1st period and also records an assist as the Oilers win 5-3 over the visiting Quebec Nordiques; his 41st NHL career hat trick
US Congress forms "Irangate" committee
Czechoslovak party leader Gustav Husak resigns
Bryan Murray becomes 17th NHL coach to win 300 games (Washington Capitals)
NY Islanders break 12 game losing streak, beat Devils 5-2
USS Tennessee, 1st sub to carry Trident 2 missiles, commissioned
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Brazil elects conservative Fernando Collor de Mello as president
KTBN, Salt Lake City Utah, begins shortwave radio transmissions
Cleveland Cavaliers beat Miami Heat 148-80, by record 68 pts
NBA's most lopsided game Cleveland beats Miami 148-80
Patrick Manning becomes premier of Trinidad & Tobago
Pittsburgh Penguins score team record 8 goals in one period in 2nd session of 10-2 win over NHL expansion San Jose Sharks
Soap opera "One Life To Live" airs its 6,000th episode
Gen Suwa finds tooth of 4.4 million year old Australopithecus ramidus
"Northern Exposure" star Barry Corbin falls off his horse
Kevin Scott skates world record 1000 m (1:12.54)
Muslims in Bangladesh call for the murder of feminist Taslima Nasrin
KLM's last DC-10 goes out of service
"In My Bed" single released by Dru Hill (Billboard Song of the Year 1997)
The United Nations General Assembly passes resolution 54/134 designating November 25 as the annual International Day to Eliminate Violence Against Women

Second Congo War: The Congolese parties of the Inter Congolese Dialogue sign a peace accord which makes provision for transitional governance and legislative and presidential elections within two years.
"The Return of the King" 3rd Lord of the Ring films is released. Makes US$72 million opening weekend, 2nd film to gross over 1 billion worldwide.
International Day to End Violence Against Sex Workers established by SWOP-USA human rights organization
SpaceShipOne flight 11P, piloted by Brian Binnie, makes its first supersonic flight
The Soham murder trial ends at the Old Bailey in London, with Ian Huntley found guilty of two counts of murder. His girlfriend Maxine Carr is found guilty of perverting the course of justice.
Anti-WTO protesters riot in Wan Chai, Hong Kong
Republic of Lakotah asserts independence from the United States
10 girls collecting firewood are killed by a mine blast in east Afghanistan
17 people are killed and 70 are injured by a blast in a market in the Khyber Agency, Pakistan
18 people drown after an overloaded boat sinks in Cotonou, Benin
Jiroemon Kimura of Japan becomes the world's oldest verified man at 116 years old
NASA completes a successful mission to map the Moon’s gravity field
Australia wins the 3rd test to take win the 2013-14 Ashes test cricket series
103-year-old Gus Andreone records the 8th ace of his career on the 113-yard, 14th hole of the Lakes Course, Palm Aire, Sarasota, FL; oldest golfer to record a hole-in-one
José Mourinho is sacked as manager of British Premier football club Chelsea
Libyan warring political factions sign a UN-brokered deal to form a unified government
Martin Shkreli, CEO of Turing Pharmaceuticals is arrested on fraud charges in New York by the FBI
Tamerlanecaptures and sacks Delhi, defeating Sultan Nasir-ud-Din Mahmud's armies by setting camels loaded with hay alight and charging them at the Sultan's armored elephants
PopeClement VIIpublishes papal bull "Cum ad nihil magis", authorizing the Inquisition into Portugal at Evora, Coimbra and Lisbon; rescinded in 1535
Pope Paul III excommunicates KingHenry VIIIof England
German explorerLudwig Leichhardtarrives in Port Essington after a nearly 4,800 km (3,000 mi) overland journey to explore Australia's Northern Territory
GeneralUlysses S. Grantissues order #11, expelling Jews from Tennessee
Franz Schubert's Symphony No. 8, "Unfinished Symphony", premieres in Vienna with Johann von Herbeck conducting
PopeLeo XIIIcloses the 'Holy Door' of St Peter's in Rome, ending the Jubilee Year of 1900
Frank Wedekind's play "Der Erdgeist" premieres in Berlin
In Turkey the new parliament convenes with reformistYoung Turksas the majority
Sultan AbdulazizIbn Saudconquers Jeddah, the last major stand of the Hashemites against the Saudis
Australian cricketing prodigyDon Bradmanscores 118 in his 1st-class debut for NSW against South Australia at the Adelaide Oval
First meeting of the Indian Statistical Institute organized byPrasanta Chandra Mahalanobisin Calcutta
St. Cardinals trade future Baseball HOF first basemanJim Bottomleyto Cincinnati Reds for Estel Crabtree & Ownie Carroll
1st flight of theDouglas DC-3 (C-47 Skytrain/Dakota) airplane.
German troops led byErwin Rommelbegin retreating in North Africa
British industrial company BTH receives a patent for holography, invented byDennis Gaborin their development lab
Dmitri Shostakovich's 10th Symphony premieres in Leningrad
Ballon d'Or: Real Madrid forwardAlfredo Di Stéfanowins award for best football player in Europe ahead of Englishmen Billy Wright and Duncan Edwards
Last episode of "TheNat King ColeShow" airs on NBC, cancelled due to lack of national sponsorship

Ted Lindsaybecomes highest scoring left wing in NHL history, passing Aurel Joliat who retired with 270; scores twice and adds an assist In Chicago Black Hawks' 5-2 win over the Boston Bruins at Chicago Stadium
"Goldfinger", 3rd James Bond film, starringSean Conneryand Honor Blackman premieres in London
1st concert event at Houston Astrodome:Judy Garlandheadlines, withThe Supremesas opening act
Elvis Costello& The Attractions 1st US TV appearance on "Saturday Night Live"

In his 352nd NHL game,Wayne Gretzkyscores a goal & 5 assists in 8-1 rout of Quebec Nordiques to record his 800th point and 500th assist; averages 2.27 points, 1.42 assists, 0.85 goals per game to start career
"A Christmas Carol" directed by Clive Donner starringGeorge C. Scottpremieres in the US
American mafia hitmanRichard Kuklinskiis arrested at a roadblock
"The Simpsons" created byMatt Groening, premieres on Fox TV as a full animated series with the episode, "Simpsons Roasting on an Open Fire"
René Prévalwins the Haiti presidential elections for the Fanmi Lavalas party
Ray Bourquebecomes just the 3rd player in NHL history to score 1,100 assists, and goaltender Byron Dafoe records his 100th NHL victory in Boston Bruin's 3-1 win over the Thrashers in Atlanta
Joe Liebermanendorses Republican SenatorJohn McCainfor president
Angela Merkelis elected Chancellor of Germany for a third term
Sony Pictures cancels the release of "The Interview", starringSeth Rogen, Lizzy Caplan, and James Franco, after a cyber attack on the studio
The Wright brothers make the first sustained motorized aircraft flight at 10:35 AM, piloted byOrville Wrightat Kitty Hawk, North Carolina
Kujō Yoritsugu, 5th Japanese shōgun of the Kamakura shogunate of Japan (1244-52), born in Kamakura, Japan (d. 1256)
Go-Uda, 91st Emperor of Japan (1274-87), born in Mansion of Tsuchimikado-dono, Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1324)
Ernest of Bavaria, Prince-elector-archbishop of the Archbishopric of Cologne, born in Munich (d. 1612)
Prince Rupert of the Rhine, English-German soldier (Royalist Commander English Civil War, 1stt Governor of the Hudson's Bay Company), born in Prague (d. 1682)
Anthony Wood, English antiquarian, born in Oxford, England (d. 1695)
Thomas Tickell, English writer and poet, born in Bridekirk, Cockermouth, England (d. 1740)
Émilie du Châtelet, French mathematician and physicist (Institutions de Physique; translation of Newton's Principia), born in Paris, Kingdom of France (d. 1749)
Gabrielle Chôtelet [La belle Emilie], French writer (Voltaire), born in Paris (d. 1749)
Georg Peter Weimar, German cantor and composer, born in Stotternheim, Electorate of Mainz, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1800)
Maria I of Portugal, Queen of Portugal and the Algarves, born in Ribeira Palace, Lisbon, Kingdom of Portugal (d. 1816)
William Floyd, American soldier who signed the US Declaration of Independence, born in Brookhaven, Province of New York (d. 1821)
Domenico Cimarosa, Italian composer (Il Matrimonio Segreto), born in Aversa, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1801)
Johann Friedrich Schubert, German composer, born in Rudolstadt, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1811)
Humphry Davy, English chemist, studied electro-chemistry, invented the Davy lamp), born in Penzance, Cornwall, England (d. 1829)
Thomas Chandler Haliburton, Canadian novelist (The Clockmaker; or, The Sayings and Doings of Samuel Slick of Slickville), born in Windsor, Canada (d. 1865)
Joseph Henry, American scientist and pioneer of electromagnetism, born in Albany, New York (d. 1878)
Gergely Czuczor, Hungarian-Czech poet and translator (Great Hung dictionary), born in Andód (d. 1866)
John Greenleaf Whittier, American Quaker poet (Snow-bound) and advocate for the abolition of slavery, born in Haverhill, Massachusetts (d. 1892)
Samuel Jones, American Major General (Confederate Army), born in Powhatan County, Virginia (d. 1887)
Frederick Tracy Dent, American General (Union Army), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1892)
Frederick West Lander, American poet and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Salem, Massachusetts (d. 1862)
John Kerr, Scottish physicist and pioneer in the field of electro-optics (electro-visually Kerr-effect), born in Ardrossan, Scotland (d. 1907)
Manning Ferguson Force, American lawyer, judge and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1899)
Thomas Starr King, American Unitarian clergyman (Christianity & Humanity), born in New York City (d. 1864)
Jules de Goncourt, French novelist (Germinie), wrote with his brother Edmond de Goncourt, born in Paris (d. 1870)
James T. Holtzclaw, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in McDonough, Georgia (d. 1893)
Alexander Agassiz, American biologist and geologist, born in Neuchâtel, Switzerland (d. 1910)
Berthold Tours, Dutch-English composer, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1897)
C. F. E. Horneman, Danish composer, born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1906)
Marius Sophus Lie, Norwegian mathematician (continuous groups), born in Nordfjordeid, Norway (d. 1899)
Émile Faguet, French writer and critic, born in La Roche-sur-Yon, France (d. 1916)
Frederick Grant Gleason, American composer, born in Middletown, Connecticut (d. 1903)
Herbert Beerbohm Tree, British actor and theatre manager (King John, Trilby), born in Kensington, London (d. 1917)
Paul César Helleu, French artist of the Belle Époque, born in Vannes, France (d. 1927)
Fritz Volbach, German conductor, musicologist and composer (Raffael), born in Wipperfürth, Germany (d. 1940)
Ion Vidu, Romanian composer and conductor, born in Mânerău, Arad district, Hungarian Kingdom (d. 1931)
(John) Felix Körling, Swedish composer and conductor, born in Kristdala, Sweden (d. 1937)
Nikolai Ivanovich Kazanli, Russian composer, born in Tiraspol, Russian Empire (d. 1916)
Ford Madox Ford [Hueffer], British novelist (The Good Soldier), critic, and journal editor (The Transatlantic), born in Merton, Surrey, England (d. 1939)
Aubrey Faulkner, South African cricket all-rounder (25 Tests, 4 x 100s, TS 204; Transvaal), born in Port Elizabeth, South Africa (d. 1930)
Jan Sluyters, Dutch painter (Moderne Kunstkring), born in s-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands (d. 1957)
Raimu [Jules Auguste Cesar Muraire], French actor (The Baker's Wife, Marius, Fanny), born in Toulon, Var, France (d. 1946)
Hermine Reuss, German second wife of Wilhelm II, former Emperor of Germany, born in Greiz, German Empire (d. 1947)
Josef Lada, Czech painter and cartoonist who illustrated "The Good Soldier Švejk", born in Hrusice, Bohemia (d. 1957)
Robertson Hare, English actor (All Gas and Gaiters. Our Girl Friday, Banana Ridge), born in London (d. 1979)
Sam Barry, American collegiate basketball, football and baseball coach (University of Southern California, 1929-50), born in Aberdeen, South Dakota (d. 1950)
Erwin Piscator, German stage director (Der Stellvertreter), born in Greifenstein-Ulm, German Empire (d. 1966)
Arthur Fiedler, American conductor (Boston Pops), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1979)
David Butler, American director (April in Paris, Calamity Jane), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1979)
Hans Henny Jahnn, German playwright, born in Stellingen, Germany (d. 1959)
Hans Kramers, Dutch theoretical physicist (quantum mechanics), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1952)
Willem Schermerhorn, Prime Minister of the Netherlands (1945-46), born in Akersloot, Netherlands (d. 1977)
Katina Paxinou, Greek actress (La femme nue), born in Piraeus, Greece (d. 1973)
Lucijan Marija Škerjajanc, Slovene pianist, composer, conductor, and teacher, born in Graz, Austria (d. 1973)
Mary Cartwright, English mathematician, born in Aynho, United Kingdom (d. 1998)
Albert Drach, Austrian-Jewish writer, born in Vienna (d. 1995)
Erskine Caldwell, American novelist (Tobacco Road, God's Little Acre), born in Moreland, Georgia (d. 1987)
Ray Noble, English jazz bandleader, songwriter ("The Very Thought of You"; "Goodnight, Sweetheart"), composer, and actor, born in Brighton, Sussex, England (d. 1978)[1]
Paul Cadmus, American etcher and painter (Sailors & Floozies), born in New York City (d. 1999)
Érico Veríssimo, Brazilian writer known for "O Tempo e o Vento", born in Cruz Alto, Brazil (d. 1975)
Iosif Kheifits [Joseph Heifitz], Belarusian-Soviet film director (Member of the Government), born in Minsk, (d. 1995)
Simo Häyhä, Finnish sniper known as 'The White Death' who killed 500 soldiers during WWII, born in Rautjärvi. Duchy of Finland, Russian Empire (d. 2002)
Fernando Lopes-Graça, Portuguese composer, conductor, and musicologist (Requiem for the Victims of Fascism in Portugal), born in Tomar, Portugal (d. 1994)[1]
William Brocklesby Wordsworth, English composer, born in London, England (d. 1988)
(Donnell) "Spade" Cooley, American Western swing fiddler, bandleader, and television personality ("Shame On You"), born in Grand Oklahoma (d. 1969)
(Melvin) "Sy" Oliver, American jazz arranger, songwriter (Jimmie Lunceford; Tommy Dorsey - "Yes, Indeed!"), and bandleader, born in Battle Creek, Michigan (d. 1988)[1]
Keki Tarapore, Indian cricketer (0-72 in his only Test for India), born in Bombay, India (d. 1986)
William Roerick, American actor (The Wasp Woman, Guiding Light), born in Hoboken, New Jersey (d. 1995)
Syed Mushtaq Ali, Indian cricketer (prolific batsman of 30's & 40's), born in Indore, British India (d. 2005)
André Claveau, French pop singer (Eurovision, 1958 - "Dors, mon amour"), and actor, born in Paris (d. 2003)
Penelope Fitzgerald, English writer known for "The Blue Flower", born in Lincoln, England (d. 2000)
Toon Hermans, Dutch poet (Kolderliedjes), born in Sittard, Netherlands (d. 2000)
Louis Palange, American arranger, classical music and film score composer (Hollywood Panorama; King Dinosaur), and conductor, born in Oakland, California (d. 1979)
Kenneth E. Iverson, Canadian computer scientist who developed APL, born in Camrose, Alberta (d. 2004)
Alan Voorhees, American engineer and urban planner, developed a model predicting traffic patterns and planned subways around the world, born in Highland Park, New Jersey (d. 2005)[1]
Jaroslav Pelikan, American historian of Christianity, born in Arkon, Ohio(d. 2006)
Sándor Lakatos, Hungarian violinist, bandleader, and actor (mostly as a gypsy violinist), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1994)
Rijk de Gooyer, Dutch actor, comedian and singer (Black Rider; Rififi in Amsterdam), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 2011)
Jeremy Brooks, English writer (Jampot Smith), born in Southampton (d. 1994)
Patrice Wymore, American actress (The Big Tree), born in Miltonvale, Kansas (d. 2014)
Stephen Lewis, British stage and screen comic actor, screenwriter and playwright (In The Buses - "Blakey"; Last of the Summer Wine - "Smiler"), born in Poplar, England (d. 2015)
Richard Long, American actor (Prof-Nanny and the Professor), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1974)
Robert Robinson, British radio and television presenter, and game show host (Ask the Family: Call My Bluff), born in Liverpool, England (d. 2011)
Eli Beeding, American Air Force Captain and rocket test pilot, born in Sherman, Texas (d. 2013)
Marilyn Beck, American newspaper columnist and entertainment reporter, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2014)
Armin Mueller-Stahl, German actor (Kafka; Music Box; Shine; Utz), painter, and, author, born in Tilsit, Germany
Julia Meade [Kunz], American actress and TV hostess (Spotlight Playhouse), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2016)
Makoto Moroi, Jpansese composer (Yamauba; The Vision of Cain), born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 2013)
Dave Madden, Canadian-born American actor (Charlotte's Web, Alice, The Partridge Family), born in Sarnia, Ontario (d. 2014)
Yvonne Keuls [Bamberg], Dutch writer (Jan Rap & Co), born in Batavia, Dutch East Indies
Paul Snoek [Edm Schietekat], Belgian poet, born in Sint-Niklaas, Belgium (d. 1981)
Stefan Florenski, Polish soccer defender (11 caps; Górnik Zabrze), born in Gleiwitz, Poland (d. 2020)
Ray Wilson, English footballer (1966 World Cup team), born in Shirebrook, England (d. 2018)
Cal Ripken Sr, American baseball manager (Baltimore Orioles 1987-88; World Series 1983 [coach]), born in Aberdeen, Maryland (d. 1999)
George Lindsey, American actor (The Andy Griffith Show), born in Fairfield, Alabama (d. 2012)
Ronnie Boykins, American jazz bassist (Sun Ra Arkestra; Mary Lou Williams; Melodic Art-Tet), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1980)
Frank Martinus Arion [Frank Efraim Martinus], Curaçaoan poet and writer (Double Play), born on Curaçao (d. 2015)
Tommy Steele [Thomas Hicks], English pop singer, 1st British Rock and Roll star, born in London
Art Neville, American funk and R&B singer, keyboard player, and songwriter (Neville Brothers; The Funky Meters), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2019)
Calvin Waller, US Army general (Persian Gulf War), born in Baton Rouge, Louisiana (d. 1996)
John Kennedy Toole, American novelist known for " A Confederacy of Dunces", born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1969)
Kerry Packer, Australian media tycoon (Nine Network), born in Sydney (d. 2005)
Carlo Little, English rock drummer (Screaming Lord Sutch & the Savages), born in Shepherd's Bush, London, England (d. 2005)
Gilles Tremblay, Canadian NHL hockey left winger, 1961-69, 4X Stanley Cup champ (Montreal Canadiens), and  French-language broadcaster, 1971-97, born in Montmorency, Quebec (d. 2014)
Peter Snell, New Zealand athlete (Olympic gold 800m 1960, 64; 1,500m 1964), born in Opunake, New Zealand (d. 2019)
Charles V. Bush, African-American pioneer, 1st African-American US Air Force Academy graduate and US Supreme Court page, born in Tallahassee, Florida (d. 2012)[1]
Eddie Kendricks, American singer-songwriter (The Temptations - "The Way You Do the Things You Do"; "Just My Imagination"), born in Union Springs, Alabama (d. 1992)
James Booker, American R&B and jazz piano player and organist ("Gonzo"), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1983)
Novella Nelson, American actress (Antwone Fisher, Purlie, Chiefs), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2017)
Kåre Valebrokk, Norwegian journalist and director of TV 2, born in Kristiansand, Norway (d. 2013)
Dave Dee [Harman], British pop singer-songwriter, and guitarist (Dave Dee, Dozy, Beaky, Mick & Tich - "Bend It!"), born in Salisbury, England (d. 2009)
David A. Gall, Canadian thoroughbred Hall of Fame jockey (US Champion Jockey by wins 1979, 81; first to ride 8 winners on single US race card), born in Rose Valley, Saskatchewan (d. 2021)
Paul Butterfield, American blues singer and harmonica player (Paul Butterfield Blues Band; Better Days), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1987)
Ron Geesin, Scottish musician and avant-garde, symphonic rock, and film score composer, born in Stevenston, Ayrshire, Scotland
William Brooks, American composer, educator, and musicologist, born in the USA
Bernard Hill, English actor (Yosser Hughes-Boys from the Blackstuff, King Théoden-The Lord of the Rings), born in Manchester, England
Ference Bene, Hungarian soccer forward (76 caps; Újpesti Dózsa 417 games; Olympic gold 1964), born in Balatonújlak, Hungary (d. 2006)
Giedrė Lukšaitė-Mrázková, Lithuanian–Czech harpsichord player, organist, and educator, born in Kaunas, Lithuania
Jack L. Chalker, American sci-fi author (Saga of Well World), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2005)
Chris Matthews, American journalist (Hardball with Chris Matthews), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
